text: A unified computational framework for single-cell data integration with optimal transport. Kai Cao, Qiyu Gong, Yiguang Hong, Lin Wan. Nature Communications, 13, 7419 (2022). Received: 15 June 2022; Accepted: 18 November 2022; Published online: 01 December 2022. Check for updates. Abstract: Single-cell data integration can provide a comprehensive molecular view of cells. However, how to integrate heterogeneous single-cell multi-omics as well as spatially resolved transcriptomic data remains a major challenge. Here we introduce uniPort, a unified single-cell data integration framework that combines a coupled variational autoencoder (coupled-VAE) and minibatch unbalanced optimal transport (Minibatch-UOT). It leverages both highly variable common and dataset-specific genes for integration to handle the heterogeneity across datasets, and it is scalable to large-scale datasets. uniPort jointly embeds heterogeneous single-cell multi-omics datasets into a shared latent space. It can further construct a reference atlas for gene imputation across datasets. Meanwhile, uniPort provides a flexible label transfer framework to deconvolute heterogeneous spatial transcriptomic data using an optimal transport plan, instead of embedding latent space. We demonstrate the capability of uniPort by applying it to integrate a variety of datasets, including single-cell transcriptomics, chromatin accessibility, and spatially resolved transcriptomic data. Introduction: The latest developments in high-throughput single-cell multi-omics sequencing technologies enable comprehensive studies of heterogeneous cell populations. The computational integration of single-cell datasets is drawing heavy attention. Among existing single-cell integration methods, tremendous efforts have been devoted to integrating multiple datasets simultaneously profiled from the same cells (e.g., paired-cell datasets generated by CITE-seq). However, these paired datasets are technically challenging and costly to obtain. Therefore, a vast number of methods have been developed for data profiled from different cells. The celebrated platform Seurat projects feature space into a common subspace using canonical correlation analysis (CCA). LIGER and DCI employed non-negative matrix factorization. Harmony iterated between maximum diversity clustering and a mixture model-based linear batch correction. However, these methods rely on linear operation, thus lacking the ability to handle nonlinear deformations across cellular modalities. In addition, they only leverage common genes and neglect dataset-specific genes, thus failing to capture cell-type heterogeneity not present in common genes. To address these shortcomings, manifold alignment methods are emerging and have achieved promising results. However, they are limited by relatively high computational complexity and are not scalable to large-scale datasets. With the development of deep learning, many autoencoder-based approaches have been proposed. However, most of them require paired datasets profiled from the same cells. When cell-paring information is unavailable, the alternative is simultaneous training of different autoencoders and aligning cells across different modalities in a latent space, but this still makes computation challenging. Recently, an emerging number of methods have been developed to account for unpaired data, but they often require global alignment which is too restrictive, or are restricted to using source modality with annotated cell labels. Recently published methods for single-cell genomics integration such as scMC and SCALEX showed state-of-the-art performance on batch effect correction of one modality, but they have not been benchmarked on single-cell multi-omics data integration. GLUE, a state-of-the-art method for single-cell multi-omics integration, is based on advanced graph autoencoders. Meanwhile, many other methods are proposed for integrative analysis of spatial transcriptomics (ST) and scRNA data, with gimVI and Tangram achieving the most advanced performance. However, to the best of our knowledge, no method has been developed for a unified integration of single-cell multi-omics as well as spatially resolved transcriptomic data. To address this gap, we develop uniPort, an accurate, robust, and efficient computational platform for integrating heterogeneous single-cell datasets with optimal transport (OT). Overview of uniPort: uniPort combines a coupled variational autoencoder (coupled-VAE) and Minibatch Unbalanced OT (Minibatch-UOT). It leverages both highly variable common and dataset-specific genes for integration. The framework allows leveraging both highly variable common and dataset-specific genes for integration to handle the heterogeneity across datasets. As input, uniPort takes diverse and heterogeneous single-cell datasets across different modalities or technologies. It is based on a coupled variational autoencoder (coupled-VAE) and employs a dataset-free encoder to project highly variable common gene sets of different datasets into a generalized cell-embedding latent space. Then uniPort reconstructs two terms. One is input by a dataset-free decoder with dataset-specific batch normalization (DSBN) layers. The other is a highly variable gene set through a dataset-specific decoder corresponding to each dataset. During integration, uniPort minimizes a Minibatch-UOT loss between cell embeddings in the latent space from different datasets. The minibatch strategy improves computational efficiency, making it scalable to large datasets, and the unbalanced OT makes it more suitable for heterogeneous data integration. uniPort outputs a shared latent space and an optimal transport plan that can be used for downstream analysis, such as visualization, gene imputation and spot deconvolution.

section: Results: Integration of scATAC and scRNA data, Unbalanced Matching, and MERFISH Integration
text: Results: uniPort embeds and integrates datasets by coupled-VAE and Minibatch-UOT. We employed a series of scores to assess performance: Batch Entropy score (evaluates mixing of cells across datasets), Silhouette coefficient (evaluates separation of biological distinctions), Adjusted Rand Index (ARI), Normalized Mutual Information (NMI), and F1 scores (for annotation clustering accuracy), and for paired datasets, average fraction of samples closer than the true match (FOSCTTM) to measure preservation of cell-cell correspondence. Integration of scATAC and scRNA data: We benchmarked uniPort against state-of-the-art methods (Seurat, LIGER, Harmony, SCOT, GLUE, etc.) on one paired PBMC dataset and two unpaired datasets (microfluidic-based PBMC and mouse spleen). For the paired PBMC dataset, uniPort and GLUE achieved the best performance with comparable results. uniPort achieved the highest Silhouette coefficient (0.64), second-best average FOSCTTM (0.0694), and a total score (ARI+NMI+F1) of 2.321. For the unpaired microfluidic-based PBMC dataset, uniPort accurately integrated data with performance comparable to GLUE, MultiMAP and Harmony. For the unpaired mouse spleen dataset, uniPort, Harmony, and Seurat achieved the highest performance. uniPort had the highest Silhouette coefficient (0.709). In summary, uniPort performed favorably across both paired and unpaired datasets. uniPort performs unbalanced matching tasks of heterogeneous datasets: To evaluate performance on heterogeneous data integration, we conducted two unbalanced matching tasks by removing some cell types from scATAC or scRNA of mouse spleen separately (UBM-ATAC and UBM-RNA). uniPort accurately identified and separated the removed cell types while still aligning modality-shared cell types well. Among all methods, only uniPort and Seurat achieved stable performance in all three cases (balanced, UBM-ATAC, UBM-RNA). uniPort is more robust than other methods when heterogeneity is present. uniPort integrates MERFISH and scRNA data: We applied uniPort to integrate MERFISH (high-plex RNA imaging-based spatial data) and scRNA data. Among 155 genes in MERFISH, 153 common genes were used. uniPort and scVI outperformed other methods in identifying and separating cell types. uniPort accurately identified ependymal cells as a MERFISH-specific cell type. uniPort outperformed other methods with the highest Silhouette coefficient (0.706) and highest total score (2.404).

section: Results: Gene Imputation, Spatial Data Deconvolution, and Applications (Mouse Brain, Breast Cancer, PDAC)
text: uniPort imputes genes for MERFISH data: Once trained, the coupled-VAE can be used as a reference atlas for gene imputation. We followed the scheme of gimVI to impute missing genes in MERFISH from scRNA. We used 80% of MERFISH genes for training and 20% for testing, repeated 12 times. uniPort provided a significant improvement over gimVI and Tangram. uniPort demonstrated the highest median/average Spearman and Pearson correlation coefficients between imputed and ground truth genes. uniPort also showed ability for online imputation by training on data from one mouse and imputing for another. uniPort deconvolutes synthetic STARmap data: For barcoding-based ST data deconvolution, uniPort transfers labels from scRNA to spots using the optimal transport plan. We applied uniPort to deconvolute synthetic STARmap data and benchmarked against Tangram and SpaOTsc using PCC, SSIM, RMSE, and JSD. uniPort performed competitively. uniPort deciphers canonical structures of mouse brain: We applied uniPort to deconvolute the anterior slice of adult mouse brain ST data (10x Visium). uniPort accurately reconstructed the well-structured cortical layers (L2/3-L6) and deconvoluted 28 cell types. The proportion and position of clusters (e.g., cortical layers, astrocytes, oligodendrocytes, VLMCs) were highly consistent with previous studies and anatomical structures. uniPort assists in locating TLS in breast cancer region: We used uniPort to deconvolute spatial data of HER2-positive breast cancer. Nine main clusters were assigned, primarily involving T cells and cancer epithelial cells. Clusters matched well with marker gene expression (e.g., CD3D for T cells, MS4A1 for B cells, CD68 for myeloid cells, ERBB2 for cancer cells). By decomposing cell-type proportions, we identified Tertiary Lymphoid Structure (TLS) signals via colocalization of T and B cells. uniPort reveals cancer heterogeneity in microarray-based spatial data: We employed microarray-based ST data of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) tissues (100 μm spot diameter) for integration. uniPort decomposed 15 main clusters, showing discrete enrichment of normal and tumor composition. Normal cell types (ductal, acinar) and malignant cells (cancer clone A and B) were identified. Histological annotations aligned with data-driven labels. Cancer clone A showed enrichment of pathways related to platelet activation and leukocyte transendothelial migration (invasive phenotype), and had a higher proportion of hematogenous cells (RBCs, T cells, NK cells). Cancer clone B showed enrichment of pathways like tight junction and PI3K signaling, and had a higher presence of endothelial cells, suggesting a less malignant cluster.

section: Discussion, Methods Part 1: uniPort Framework and Algorithm
text: Discussion: uniPort is a unified deep learning method for single-cell data integration, combining a coupled-VAE and Minibatch-UOT. It leverages both common and dataset-specific genes. It removes the constraint of paired cells required by other autoencoder-based models. It shows power for gene imputation by constructing a reference atlas. It can output an OT plan for downstream analysis like label transfer and deconvolution. uniPort is computationally efficient and scalable to large-scale datasets, unlike prevalent OT-based methods that use global OT. The minibatch strategy significantly reduces computational cost. uniPort consistently performs favorably compared to state-of-the-art methods. We also tested uniPort's performance with different gene activity score calculation methods (Signac, MAESTRO) for scATAC integration; performance was better with MAESTRO. uniPort demonstrated generalizability to other data types (CITE-seq, SNARE-seq) and datasets without aligned common genes. The framework offers flexibility to incorporate prior information (e.g., cell-type annotations) when available. Methods: uniPort framework. uniPort inputs each dataset through a coupled variational autoencoder (coupled-VAE) framework. Given a sample x, the corresponding K-dimensional latent vector z is obtained by a variational posterior p(z|x) approximated by a probabilistic encoder ψ(z|x). A probabilistic decoder φ(x|z) produces a distribution over x given z. The coupled-VAE maximizes the evidence lower bound (ELBO): ℒ_ELBO = E_ψ(z|x)[log φ(x|z)] - λ D_KL(ψ(z|x) || p(z)). (1) The prior p(z) is a centered isotropic multivariate Gaussian; the variational posterior is a multivariate Gaussian with diagonal covariance: ψ(z|x) = N(z; μ, σ^2 I). The latent vector is obtained by reparameterization: z = μ + σ × v, where v ~ N(0, I). Suppose there are two datasets X (n_x cells, d_x genes) and Y (n_y cells, d_y genes). We select the top k highly variable common genes to form X^c and Y^c, and the top k_x and k_y highly variable genes in X and Y to form X^s and Y^s. We project X^c and Y^c into a latent space using a shared encoder ψ and a decoder φ with two Dataset-Specific Batch Normalization (DSBN) layers. We also introduce two decoders, φ_x and φ_y, to reconstruct X^s and Y^s. The modified ELBO' loss is: ℒ_ELBO' = E_ψ(z|x){log φ(x|z) + λ_s (log φ_x(x^s|z) + log φ_y(y^s|z))} - λ D_KL(ψ(z|x) || p(z)), (2) where x ∈ X^c ∪ Y^c, x^s ∈ X^s, y^s ∈ Y^s, and λ_s, λ are balanced parameters. To align datasets, we use Minibatch Unbalanced Optimal Transport (Minibatch-UOT). For two K-dimensional Gaussian distributions p_{x_i} = N(μ_{x_i}, σ_{x_i}^2 I) and p_{y_j} = N(μ_{y_j}, σ_{y_j}^2 I) corresponding to cells x_i and y_j, the Minibatch-UOT cost is: C_{ij} = ||μ_{x_i} - μ_{y_j}||^2 + ||σ_{x_i} - σ_{y_j}||^2. (3) The optimal Minibatch-UOT plan T* (with batch sizes B_x, B_y) is: T* = argmin_{T∈R_{+}^{B_x×B_y}} <C, T> - ε H(T) + ρ D_KL(T 1_{B_y} || a) + ρ D_KL(T^⊤ 1_{B_x} || b), (4) where <C,T> = Σ_{i,j} C_{ij} T_{ij}, H(T) = -Σ_{i,j} T_{ij}(log T_{ij} - 1) is entropy regularization, a = (1/B_x) 1_{B_x}, b = (1/B_y) 1_{B_y}, and ε, ρ are balanced parameters (ρ=1). Eq.(4) is solved via an efficient inexact proximal point method (IPOT). The optimal plan T*_{ij} = α_i G_{ij} β_j. The Minibatch-UOT loss is: ℒ_UOT* = Σ_{i,j} C_{ij} × T*_{ij}. (6) The total loss is: ℒ_uniPort = -ℒ_ELBO' + γ ℒ_UOT*. Once T* is obtained, an OT plan T can be output for tasks needing cell-to-cell probabilistic correspondence. uniPort algorithm: Input: top k highly variable common genes (X^c, Y^c), top k_x, k_y highly variable genes (X^s, Y^s). Output: aligned latent vectors z_x, z_y, and an optimal transport plan T. Steps: 1. Initialize coupled-VAE encoder ψ and decoders φ, φ_x, φ_y, and an OT plan T (optional). 2. For m = 1,..., M: a. Randomly sample minibatches I (size B_x) from X and J (size B_y) from Y. b. Initialize Minibatch-UOT plan T_B by sampling rows/columns of T corresponding to I, J, or uniform. c. Input batches through encoder ψ to get (μ_x, σ_x), (μ_y, σ_y); reparameterize to get z_x, z_y. d. Reconstruct X^c, Y^c by decoder φ, and X^s, Y^s by decoders φ_x, φ_y. e. Compute minibatch transport cost C via Eq.(3) and obtain optimal Minibatch-UOT plan T* via Eq.(4). f. Fix T* and update ψ, φ, φ_x, φ_y via backpropagation by minimizing ℒ_UOT* and -ℒ_ELBO'. g. Update T with rows/columns as T*_B corresponding to I, J (optional).

section: Methods Part 2: Training, Preprocessing, Compared Methods, Evaluation Metrics, Data & Code
text: Training details: uniPort consists of one encoder (two-layer neural network, 1024-128, ReLU) and three decoders (no hidden layer, directly connect latent variables to output with Sigmoid). Adam optimizer with weight decay 5e-4. Minibatch size 256. Learning rate 2e-4. OT entropy regularization parameter ε=0.1. Parameters λ ∈ {0.5,1.0,5.0}, γ ∈ {0.5,1.0}, λ_s ∈ {0.5,1.0}. Robust to different choices. Default max training iterations 30,000 with early stopping. Data preprocessing: Detailed preprocessing for each dataset (human PBMC, microfluidic PBMC, mouse spleen, synthetic STARmap, brain scRNA & MERFISH, 10x Visium mouse brain & breast cancer, PDAC) is described. General uniPort preprocessing: (1) Filter cells with <200 genes and genes in <3 cells (for PBMC). (2) Normalize total counts per cell. (3) log-normalize with offset 1. (4) Identify k=2000 highly variable common genes across all datasets, and k_x=k_y=2000 highly variable genes for each dataset. (5) Normalize each gene to 0-1 within each dataset. Settings used in comparing methods: Benchmarked against Seurat, Liger, Harmony, SCALEX, MultiMAP, scVI, scMC, GLUE, SCOT, Pamona, Tangram, gimVI. Used default or recommended parameters as per tutorials. Evaluation metrics: Cell-type assigning accuracy: Adjusted Rand Index (ARI), Normalized Mutual Information (NMI), F1 score. Cluster separation and batch mixing: Silhouette coefficient (scaled 0-1), Batch Entropy score. For paired datasets: average Fraction of Samples Closer Than the True Match (average FOSCTTM). Formulas provided. Data availability: All data are publicly available; links in Supplementary Data 1. Code availability: The uniPort Python package is available via PyPI (https://pypi.org/project/uniport/) and GitHub (https://github.com/caokai1073/uniPort).
